output:
# DRIVE — Drivability-Regularized Inversion for Volumetric Expressive Avatars

**Here is a high-level summarized insight of a research field Machine Learning.**

Animatable, expressive whole-body 3D human avatars built from a single *casually captured monocular video* are a rapidly maturing frontier of neural rendering, fusing parametric body models (SMPL-X) with efficient photorealistic primitives (3D Gaussian Splatting). The field's promise is an avatar that can be *driven* by arbitrary novel facial-expression codes and body/hand poses — yet its dominant success metric is photometric fidelity (PSNR/SSIM/LPIPS) on *held-out frames of the very same video*. Those held-out frames are drawn from the **same training distribution** of expressions and poses, so the metric silently rewards an avatar that *memorizes and bakes* the observed configurations rather than one that *generalizes* to unseen drives. The true desideratum — faithful, artifact-free rendering under **out-of-distribution (OOD) novel expressions, hand poses, body poses, and occluded regions** — is precisely what casual monocular capture under-constrains (limited expression/pose diversity; no 3D observations), and precisely what current in-distribution evaluation fails to isolate. This is the **Drivability Gap**.

**Here are the five core questions:**

## [Question 1] - What is the problem?

How can we build an expressive whole-body 3D human avatar from a single casual monocular video that is *robustly drivable* out-of-distribution — producing plausible, identity-consistent, artifact-free renders under novel facial-expression codes, hand poses, body poses, and across regions left occluded or unobserved during capture — and measure this drivability directly, rather than conflating it with in-distribution photometric reconstruction of held-out frames from the same video?

## [Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?

Expressive whole-body avatars built from a single casual video (ExAvatar, Moon et al., ECCV 2024) are the enabling technology for the next decade of **telepresence, AR/VR social presence, digital humans, film/previs, and games**: anyone with a phone clip could become a fully drivable, photorealistic, hand- and face-expressive 3D self. The bottleneck for every one of these applications is *not* how faithfully the avatar replays the captured clip — it is whether it stays artifact-free when **driven by novel expressions, novel MANO hand poses, and novel body poses it never observed**, including correctly hallucinated occluded regions. That is exactly the gap a teleconference reveals the moment a user smiles in a way the capture never saw, or rotates a wrist the camera never imaged.

**Why a drivability-first reframing matters more than another fidelity bump.** The monocular-avatar field measures progress with photometric fidelity (PSNR/SSIM/LPIPS) on **held-out frames of the *same* video** — frames drawn from the *same* expression/pose distribution as training. We argue this is a **confound** `[conjectured]`: it rewards an avatar that *memorizes and bakes* the observed expressions and hand poses, while the true desideratum of an *animatable* asset is **out-of-distribution (OOD) drivability**. Naming and measuring this — the **Drivability Gap (DG): In-Distribution Fidelity ≠ Out-of-Distribution Drivability** `[designed]` — operationalizes ExAvatar's own two stated challenges (limited expression/pose diversity; occlusion ambiguity from no 3D observations) into something a benchmark can *score*. A fidelity bump on the old metric can leave the DG untouched, or even widen it via overfitting; only a metric that *isolates extrapolation* tells the community whether avatars are getting more drivable.

**How it changes how the field evaluates avatars.** DRIVE proposes a **new benchmark axis** the community can adopt: drive the avatar with parameters **disjoint from training** (cross-sequence and cross-identity expression transfer, extreme FLAME/MANO sweeps) and score against **multi-view / 3D ground truth**, adding identity preservation, expression accuracy, hand-pose fidelity, and occluded-region plausibility — while still reporting the standard in-distribution number to prove **do-no-harm** `[conjectured, measured non-inferiority within ε]`. This is a measurement contribution, not just a method: if adopted, it **shifts research effort from in-distribution overfitting toward extrapolation and occlusion plausibility**, the properties that actually decide whether an avatar is usable downstream.

**Practical applications enabled.** A measurable DG directly serves immersive telepresence (faithful hands for sign and gesture, faces for affect), embodied AR/VR identity, virtual production and game-character capture from set footage, and accessibility tools — all of which fail on *driving*, not on *replay*.

**Epistemic honesty.** Our regularizer is a **mesh-anchored diffusion (SDS) prior** that *hallucinates* plausible appearance for unseen drives and occluded regions. Improvements are **measured perceptually, not guaranteed faithful** `[conjectured→measured]`: a synthesized occluded tattoo or scar can be plausible yet **wrong**. We claim only *plausible, identity-consistent* completion — never recovery of the true unseen appearance — and we measure it rather than assert it.

## [Question 3] - Why is it hard?

The Drivability Gap is hard precisely because each piece that *looks* easy hides a confound, an ill-posed inverse problem, or a circularity that naive approaches walk straight into.

- **Measurement hardness (no OOD ground truth for a casual subject).** `[designed]` For a person filmed once on a phone, there is *no* recorded ground truth of how they look under a novel expression, an unseen hand grasp, or an extreme body pose. The field's standard fix — "just hold out frames" — fails by construction: held-out frames of the same video are sampled from the *same* expression/pose distribution, so they are **in-distribution**. The metric that is supposed to certify drivability instead rewards an avatar that *memorizes and bakes* the observed configurations. The instrument hides the very gap it claims to measure. `[conjectured→measured]` Closing this requires importing **external** multi-view/3D ground truth (X-Humans, DNA-Rendering, RenderMe-360, Multiface) at *novel* drives as an Oracle — which introduces a cross-distribution (and cross-identity) shift that the metric must explicitly account for rather than assume away.

- **Ill-posedness / occlusion ambiguity.** A monocular video with limited expression diversity under-constrains both occluded geometry/appearance (back of the head, armpits, inner hands, regions never seen) and unobserved expression/pose configurations. A pure photometric fit is free to explain the *observed* pixels with off-manifold geometry — it overfits the seen configs and produces tearing, smearing, and floaters the moment it is driven off-distribution. Adding capacity makes this *worse*, not better: more degrees of freedom fit the seen frames more tightly while leaving the unseen manifold even less constrained.

- **Why a diffusion prior is non-trivial.** `[designed]` Score Distillation Sampling (SDS) is the obvious tool for hallucinating the unseen, but it is high-variance and mode-seeking; left unconstrained it washes out identity, over-saturates, and injects **plausible-but-wrong** detail (a hallucinated tattoo, scar, or hairline). Two coupling problems make it harder: (i) a 2D image prior must drive a **3D mesh-bound Gaussian field** with a *deformation-consistent* signal, so gradients at one sampled pose do not corrupt geometry at another; (ii) the prior must be **anchored** to SMPL-X topology and **gated** so it improves OOD drivability *without* regressing in-distribution fidelity (do-no-harm). Naively maximizing a prior score and minimizing photometric loss are in tension; the schedule, anchoring (ControlNet on SMPL-X normal/depth renders), and gating are where the difficulty actually lives.

- **Disentanglement.** Faithful drivability requires separating *expression/hand/body drive* from *baked appearance/lighting*. Monocular SMPL-X registration (Hand4Whole/OSX/SMPLer-X) carries non-trivial error, especially for fingers and face; that error is silently absorbed into appearance during fitting and then re-emerges as artifacts when the drive changes. Extreme FLAME/MANO codes can leave the natural-human manifold entirely, so "novel drive" and "invalid drive" must be distinguished.

- **Confound / circularity honesty.** `[conjectured→measured]` The prior that repairs occlusion is itself trained on *external* humans, so it can import demographic bias (hallucinating majority-group appearance into occluded regions) and **plausibility is not faithfulness** — a perceptually convincing reconstruction can be biometrically wrong. We do not assume these away: they are surfaced as *testable* (identity-leakage audit, fairness slices, FID/KID of revealed regions, Placebo falsifier), consistent with Q1's framing of drivability as **measured plausibility, not guaranteed faithful recovery**.

**Why it is nonetheless tractable.** `[designed]`/`[provable-by-construction]` The hybrid SMPL-X-mesh + 3DGS scaffold constrains geometry (connectivity + as-rigid-as-possible/Laplacian regularization), so the prior only needs to supply *appearance* on a topology it cannot tear. The three boundary cases (Axis C) are built-in sanity checks: λ→0 must reproduce ExAvatar, so any regression is immediately visible. And external multi-view datasets provide a genuine **Oracle** ground truth at novel drives, turning an unmeasurable desideratum into a quantifiable benchmark.

## [Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?

The problem sits in the blind spot of five prior-art clusters: each made a choice that, individually reasonable, jointly prevented anyone from *naming, measuring, and regularizing* out-of-distribution drivability for a casually-captured expressive avatar. We organize the clusters, state the barrier each leaves standing, and then differentiate DRIVE.

**Cluster 1 — Monocular NeRF/volumetric body avatars.** HumanNeRF (Weng et al., CVPR 2022), Vid2Avatar (Guo et al., CVPR 2023), Neural Body (Peng et al., CVPR 2021), InstantAvatar (Jiang et al., CVPR 2023), Anim-NeRF/ARAH, and MonoHuman (Yu et al., CVPR 2023) reconstruct an animatable *body* from monocular video but **bake facial expression and hand pose** and are evaluated **in-distribution** on held-out frames. *Barrier left standing:* no expressive face/hand drive at all, and the evaluation never probes novel expressions.

**Cluster 2 — 3DGS avatars.** GaussianAvatar (Hu et al., CVPR 2024), 3DGS-Avatar (Qian et al., CVPR 2024), Animatable Gaussians (Li et al., CVPR 2024), GauHuman (Hu et al., CVPR 2024), and HUGS (Kocabas et al., CVPR 2024) gain photorealistic, efficient rendering but treat Gaussians as an **unconnected point cloud** (no mesh connectivity) and again report **in-distribution** PSNR/SSIM/LPIPS. *Barrier:* without surface topology there is no clean scaffold to anchor a generative prior for unseen drives, and the metric still hides extrapolation.

**Cluster 3 — Expressive avatars needing 3D observations.** X-Avatar (Shen et al., CVPR 2023), with the **X-Humans** dataset, delivers SMPL-X-expressive whole-body avatars but **requires 3D scans or RGB-D** with diverse poses/expressions — the very assumption that fails for casual video. ExAvatar (Moon et al., ECCV 2024) *removes* the 3D-observation assumption via its **hybrid mesh–Gaussian** representation (each Gaussian a SMPL-X vertex with pre-defined triangle connectivity), but it still **optimizes photometric fit to the clip** and explicitly states the two challenges DRIVE attacks: limited expression/pose diversity, and occlusion ambiguity from no 3D observations. *Barrier:* dropping 3D supervision reintroduces exactly the OOD ambiguity that an in-distribution objective cannot resolve.

**Cluster 4 — SDS / diffusion 3D priors.** DreamFusion/Score Distillation Sampling (Poole et al., ICLR 2023) and SDS-based **avatar-from-TEXT** creation — DreamAvatar (Cao et al., 2023), AvatarBooth (Zeng et al., 2023), HumanGaussian (Liu et al., CVPR 2024), GAvatar (Yuan et al., CVPR 2024) — distill a generative prior to **create a STATIC identity from a text prompt**. They do **not** regularize the **animation-time extrapolation of a *real, captured* person**, and they are **not mesh-topology-anchored** for whole-body expression/hand drives. *Barrier:* the community used SDS for *creation*, not for *constraining drives* of an existing reconstruction — a different objective entirely.

**Cluster 5 — Avatar evaluation/benchmarks.** Existing avatar evaluations report in-distribution PSNR/SSIM/LPIPS on **held-out frames of the same sequence**; none isolate **OOD expression/pose extrapolation** as the *primary* axis. *Barrier:* the field's own yardstick conceals the gap.

**The three barriers, stated plainly.**
- **(a) OOD ground-truth barrier.** There is no standard way to obtain *out-of-distribution* ground truth for a casually-captured subject — you cannot ask a phone clip for the frames it never recorded. This forces a **cross-dataset / cross-identity protocol** using multi-view studio captures (X-Humans; DNA-Rendering, ICCV 2023; RenderMe-360; Multiface; Goliath/Ava-256) as an oracle, which prior monocular work never assembled.
- **(b) Measurement barrier.** The dominant metric — held-out *same-distribution* frames — **structurally hides** drivability failures, so there was no incentive (or instrument) to optimize for them. You cannot fix what your benchmark cannot see.
- **(c) Representation + objective barrier.** Combining **mesh-anchored hybrid Gaussians** with an **SDS prior applied to *animation extrapolation*** (rendering the avatar under *sampled novel SMPL-X drives* during training and pulling them toward the human-image manifold) is neither static-creation SDS nor in-distribution fitting; nobody had married the topology scaffold to a drive-time generative objective.

**How DRIVE differs and improves.** DRIVE (i) **inverts the confound** by defining and measuring the **Drivability Gap** as a first-class axis with multi-view/3D oracles and a leakage-controlled, cross-identity split `[designed]`; (ii) **regularizes unseen expression/pose and occluded regions with a mesh-anchored diffusion (SDS) prior** that uses ExAvatar's SMPL-X topology as the scaffold and the prior as appearance for unseen drives `[designed]`, directly targeting ExAvatar's two stated challenges `[conjectured→measured]`; and (iii) is **falsifiable by construction** — λ→0 recovers ExAvatar (pure photometric fit), mesh-connectivity→0 recovers free 3DGS point-cloud avatars, and rigid binding recovers a textured SMPL-X mesh `[provable-by-construction]` — with a Placebo (shuffled-expression / un-anchored prior) that must **not** improve OOD drivability. We do **not** claim DRIVE recovers the *true* unseen appearance; the prior hallucinates **plausibly and identity-consistently**, and this is **measured, not asserted** `[conjectured→measured]`. The point is not that prior work was wrong — each cluster solved its chosen problem — but that no one had simultaneously *named* the gap, *built the oracle to see it*, and *anchored a drive-time prior* to close it.

## [Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?

**Method.** `[designed]`
1. **Base hybrid avatar.** Per-subject fit of a SMPL-X-mesh + 3D Gaussian Splatting representation to the monocular video, where each Gaussian is a vertex on the SMPL-X surface with pre-defined triangle connectivity (following ExAvatar). Training loss = photometric (RGB) + foreground mask + mesh regularizers (as-rigid-as-possible / Laplacian smoothness) to keep deformation on-manifold. SMPL-X parameters per frame come from existing whole-body fitters (Hand4Whole / OSX / SMPLer-X).
2. **Mesh-anchored SDS regularizer (Axis B).** During training we additionally sample **novel SMPL-X drives** — expressions, MANO hand poses, and body poses drawn from a pose/expression *bank disjoint from the training video* — render the avatar under those drives, and apply **score distillation** from a pretrained image diffusion model (optionally human/face-specialized), **ControlNet-conditioned on the SMPL-X normal/depth render** so the prior is anchored to the correct geometry and pose. This pulls occluded regions and unseen configurations toward the natural-human image manifold without inventing new geometry.
3. **Do-no-harm gating.** `[conjectured, measured non-inferiority within ε]` The SDS term is weighted/scheduled and gated so in-distribution held-out fidelity stays **non-inferior within ε** to the λ→0 base; the prior is allowed to act only where photometric evidence is absent (occlusion / novel drive).
4. **Optional 3D-aware variant.** A multi-view / 3D-aware diffusion prior in place of single-image SDS, for stronger cross-view consistency.

**Boundary cases (Axis C).** `[provable-by-construction]` λ→0 recovers **ExAvatar** (pure photometric per-subject fit); mesh-connectivity→0 recovers a **free 3DGS point-cloud avatar**; rigid face/body binding with no per-Gaussian appearance freedom recovers a **textured SMPL-X mesh**. These are ablations-by-construction and built-in falsifiers.

**Datasets.** `[designed]`
- *Monocular inputs:* ExAvatar's own captures, NeuMan, in-the-wild internet clips.
- *OOD multi-view ground truth (Oracle):* **X-Humans** (X-Avatar), **DNA-Rendering**, **RenderMe-360**, **ZJU-MoCap** (body), **Multiface** (face), plus Goliath/Ava-256 if available; **FLAME/MANO extreme parameter sweeps** for boundary drives.

**Metrics.** `[designed]`
- **OOD-drivability fidelity:** PSNR / SSIM / LPIPS at *novel* drives vs. multi-view GT.
- **Identity preservation:** face-recognition cosine (e.g., ArcFace) under novel expression.
- **Expression accuracy:** 3D-landmark / Action-Unit / emotion-classifier consistency vs. the target code.
- **Hand-pose fidelity:** MANO keypoint reprojection error.
- **Occluded-region plausibility:** FID / KID of newly revealed regions (explicitly *plausibility*, not faithfulness).
- **In-distribution held-out fidelity:** standard same-video numbers as a **no-regression / do-no-harm** check.
- **User study** on perceived realism and identity match.

**Evaluation ladder (Oracle / Placebo / Recovery / Leakage).** `[designed]`
- **Oracle** = multi-view captured GT at the novel drives = upper bound.
- **Placebo** (central falsifier) = the prior fed **shuffled / mismatched expression codes**, or an SDS prior with **no SMPL-X anchoring**, **MUST NOT** improve OOD drivability. If it does, gains are mere extra capacity/regularization, not real extrapolation.
- **Recovery** = λ→0 reproduces ExAvatar's held-out-frame numbers (boundary sanity).
- **Leakage** = novel-drive split disjoint from training frames (cross-sequence / cross-identity); diffusion prior **not** trained or tuned on the test subject; explicit identity-leakage audit.

**Baselines.** ExAvatar (primary), GaussianAvatar / 3DGS-Avatar / Animatable Gaussians, HumanNeRF / Vid2Avatar, and X-Avatar (near-oracle, since it consumes 3D observations).

**Expected outcomes.** `[conjectured→measured]` Substantial gains over ExAvatar and 3DGS-point-cloud baselines on **OOD-drivability fidelity, occluded-region plausibility, and identity preservation** under novel expression/hand/body drives, while **in-distribution held-out fidelity stays non-inferior within ε** `[conjectured, measured non-inferiority within ε]`. The **Placebo shows no gain**, and the three **boundary cases recover** their respective prior methods `[provable-by-construction]`. **MEASURED-NOT-ASSERTED:** the prior produces *plausible, identity-consistent* hallucinations of occluded/unseen appearance — measured perceptually, **never claimed as faithful recovery** of the true unseen detail.

**Ethics & Responsible Use.** Photorealistic drivable avatars from casual video are **biometric/identity data** and carry **deepfake, impersonation, and non-consensual-animation** risk. (1) *Consent, provenance & revocation:* we require **subject consent and provenance** for every capture, store source videos and fitted avatars under **access control with a documented retention limit and an explicit right to revoke/delete** the avatar and source clip, and use all human-subject datasets (X-Humans, DNA-Rendering, RenderMe-360, ZJU-MoCap, Multiface, Goliath/Ava-256) **strictly under their data-use agreements** with no cross-dataset identity re-release. (2) *Disclosure:* every synthesized animation is tagged with an open provenance standard (**C2PA content credentials**) plus an embedded watermark, so it is disclosable as AI-generated and traceable. (3) *Fairness:* because the external prior can hallucinate majority-group appearance into occluded regions, we report **fairness slices across skin tone (Fitzpatrick/Monk), gender presentation, and age**, disaggregating identity and plausibility metrics per slice — and note that **ArcFace cosine and FID/KID are themselves demographically biased**, so scores are interpreted per-slice, never as a single aggregate. (4) *Misuse limitation:* cross-identity expression transfer is used **only as an evaluation oracle on consented subjects**; we do not release tooling to puppeteer a captured person with a third party's motion without that person's consent. (5) *Downstream non-use:* because occluded detail is **hallucinated (plausible but possibly false)**, outputs **must not be used for forensic, medical, or identity-verification purposes**, and downstream users are warned that scars/tattoos/medical signs may be plausible yet false.
